title: Hackers Claim to Leak Stolen Madison Square Garden Data
gist: A weekly security news roundup covering: ShinyHunters hacking group publishing 45GB of alleged Madison Square Garden data triggering a federal class-action lawsuit; Meta testing military-grade face recognition from Rank One for its smart glasses; Anthropic still negotiating with the Trump administration after being forced to pull Claude Fable 5; France's domestic spy agency dropping Palantir for French alternative ChapsVision; and San Francisco gay bars using facial recognition from Patronscan to collect customer data and build shared 'safety networks.'

themes.theme: ShinyHunters MSG breach and litigation cascade

insights.claim: ShinyHunters published 45 gigabytes of data allegedly stolen from Madison Square Garden—including customer personal information and references to Knicks players and coaches—triggering a federal class-action lawsuit shortly after the Knicks won their first NBA championship since 1973.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: The breach-to-lawsuit timeline is shortening; companies with extensive surveillance infrastructure (like MSG's known facial recognition systems) face amplified liability when that data is compromised.
insights.quote: The published data, allegedly comprising millions of records across 45 gigabytes of files, includes potential personal information from customers, and references players and coaches from the Knicks.

insights.claim: ShinyHunters has claimed a string of recent high-profile victims including education tech firm Instructure (disrupting thousands of schools), photography firm Kodak, and a key European human rights organization—demonstrating the group is operating at scale across sectors.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: ShinyHunters' cross-sector attack pattern increases insurance and cybersecurity spending urgency for mid-market enterprises; Instructure (Canvas LMS) disruption signals EdTech as a soft target.

themes.theme: Meta smart glasses face recognition expansion

insights.claim: Meta is testing face-recognition software built by Rank One—a company that supplies the US military and regional police—for potential integration into its smart glasses, despite having previously deleted code from the glasses app that would have enabled facial recognition.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Meta (META) is pursuing covert-to-overt expansion of facial recognition on consumer wearables; regulatory backlash risk is high given Rank One's law enforcement pedigree and prior code deletion suggesting deliberate concealment.
insights.quote: Meta is testing face-recognition software built by Rank One, a company that supplies the US military and regional police departments, WIRED found in an investigation this week.

insights.claim: France's domestic spy agency DGSI is dropping Palantir's data and AI tools in favor of French firm ChapsVision's software, following Germany's BfV making the same switch the prior month—part of a broader European government exodus from US technology citing surveillance and security risks.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Palantir (PLTR) faces accelerating loss of European government intelligence contracts; the multi-country ChapsVision pattern suggests a structural, not idiosyncratic, shift—watch for more EU agency defections.
insights.quote: France's domestic spy agency, the Direction Générale de la Sécurité Intérieure, announced it would stop using Palantir's data and AI tools in the coming years, replacing them with software from French firm ChapsVision.

insights.claim: French Prime Minister Lecornu explicitly invoked technological sovereignty in announcing the Palantir replacement: 'We must use our own AI models. We cannot rely on tools developed by foreign powers. France must have its own tools.'—signaling this is a political, not purely technical, decision.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Tech sovereignty doctrine is now operational policy in European intelligence; US defense/intelligence tech vendors (Palantir, Microsoft, Booz Allen's European operations) face structurally elevated contract loss risk.
insights.quote: We must use our own AI models. We cannot rely on tools developed by foreign powers. France must have its own tools.

insights.claim: At least three San Francisco Castro District gay bars are using Patronscan's face-scanning technology at entry to collect customers' facial images, names, and genders, then sharing flagged individuals across a 'safety network' of other participating venues—creating a private biometric database for bar patrons.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Commercial venue face recognition is normalizing without regulatory guardrails in California; privacy backlash risk and potential CCPA exposure for Patronscan and its venue clients.

themes.theme: Apple privacy tool undermined by domain change

insights.claim: Apple is changing its 'Hide My Email' privacy feature so that generated addresses will use the domain @private.icloud.com instead of @icloud.com, making it easier for websites and apps to detect and block the privacy-preserving tool, effectively reducing its protection for users who rely on it.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Apple's privacy tools are valuable marketing assets but technically fragile; this domain change weakens a key privacy differentiator, potentially eroding user trust in Apple's privacy-first positioning.
insights.quote: the not-so-subtle change could make it easier for firms to detect when people are using the privacy-preserving service and demand sign-ups with a different email address.
